title: RA.1014 Wax'o Paradiso

translation.content: Once asked where they got the name for their joint project, Simon TK and Edd Fisher responded casually. "We were asked to produce a record fair many moons ago," they said. "Wax'o Paradiso was the name of that event." Happy accident or not, the name rings true more than a decade later. What started as a record fair has since evolved into an event series and now a record label, with the duo becoming pillars of Naarm (Melbourne)'s local scene in the meantime, crafting paradisiacal musical worlds wherever they go. Wax'o Paradiso are a live act first and foremost. Rarely relying on conventional venues, their parties have become the stuff of urban lore, held everywhere from Fairfield Amphitheatre to an old convent and even a children's farm. Often outdoors, these gatherings carry a breezy, open-hearted feeling, and that flows directly into their sets. Think of it as Australian Balearic: eclectic explorations through warm, rolling shades of dance music, laced with licks of '90s prog and tech house, the spiritual foundation of any good bush doof. RA.1014 captures why the pair have become stalwarts of their local circuit. It doesn't recreate those beloved open-air sessions, but at nearly three hours long, it settles around you like a summer's night—and, for listeners in colder climates, perhaps a bittersweet reminder of one. Most tellingly, it's bookended by tracks from two Australian artists across two generations. Ten years in, Wax'o Paradiso's focus remains steady: build community, elevate local voices and share the eclectic, joyful sound that has made them a cornerstone of Naarm's scene. Their RA Mix is a clear extension of that mission: warm, generous and rooted in the place they call home. -Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Right now we're launching into the Australian summer festival season. The doof scene has been thriving this year which is great to see! We've both come back from a Europe trip inspired and refreshed, [it was] nice to head to the northern hemisphere and link with friends and play some shows. Since then we've been warming up, starting to play more again locally and have both been quite busy with our own projects outside of Wax'o Paradiso. Edd has recently taken over Naarm/Melbourne nightclub Angel Music Bar, and Simon runs Skylab Radio, alongside our DJ schedule, label and respective radio commitments. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? We're so honoured to be asked to do the RA Mix, it's been a dream of ours. It was nice to create a snapshot of where we're at musically in a non-live setting. We play in a very live style and tend to publish live recordings as they capture us at our most comfortable. We don't often sit in the studio and practice sets; improvising live in front of an audience is usually the most authentic version of us. So our goal was to encapsulate as much as possible the essence of the eclecticism and joy that we try to convey through our parties. Preparing for this mix was a lot of fun. We wanted to reference the influence that Balearic culture has had on us and play a diverse palette of sounds. We start with some local music on our label, then head into some percussive, drifting and floaty tech house, dip a toe into prog and some heaters as well. We also feel very strongly about bookending the mix with local music from two different generations. In the end, we decided to go all digital for the actual recording of this one, which meant a lot of hours ripping and mastering records. Simon has a setup at home that we've been honing together for a few years, and with the help of Naarm-based audio wizard Morgan Wright, we've been able to achieve the right formula for digitising our records for the clubs, festivals and big rigs for the big summer ahead. The mix was recorded at Simon's home in Thornbury, with two CDJ3000s, our beloved custom-made Condesa Carmen SE mixer (shout out Mehdi and Lauren Hansom) and a BOSS RE-20 delay pedal. Special thanks to Morgan Wright for mastering this for us. What are you looking forward to in the near future? We're about to play our #1 bucket-list gig: a closing sunrise set at Meredith Music Festival. It's a special party in the Meredith supernatural amphitheatre and the single-stage event is etched into local folklore, especially the sunrise set for the DJs. Aside from that, we're returning to the beach stage at Strawberry Fields, one of our most-played festivals and favourite ever settings to play. We're also planning our own summer open-air series here in Naarm/Melbourne and some club shows that kick off next month. Summer at home is always busy but it's an exciting time of year for parties and hopefully some time in nature or at the beach in there somewhere. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? The ongoing genocide and unlawful occupation of Palestine, and the effects of settler colonisation on Indigenous peoples around the world. Especially in the context of so-called Australia's own colonial history, we think it's important to view the issue not as isolated incidents but as symptomatic of larger systematic systems of oppression and violence. We stand in solidarity with Palestinian people, First Nations people in so-called Australia and Indigenous peoples all around the world. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Otologic and the Mercat for showing us eclecticism in a truly inspiring way; Katie Pearson's Outpost; Inner Varnika for setting the bar for a one-stage festival; Hugs&kisses for bending the rules; and most recently Miscellania.
translation.blurb: A sun-soaked broadcast from two of Naarm's favourite selectors.
